explanations.87: On error Resume Next tells the script to keep going even if an error happens on the next line, so we can check if Excel is already running.
explanations.89: GetObject with no first parameter tries to find an already-running Excel application instead of starting a new one.
explanations.91: This checks both if there was an error (Err.number <> 0) and if we somehow still don't have an Excel object, covering both failure cases.
explanations.101: Err.clear resets the error tracking so old errors don't interfere with future error checking.
explanations.103: On error Goto 0 turns off the error-ignoring mode we started on line 87, so errors will cause problems again if they happen.
explanations.109: The function returns whether Excel was freshly started by this script, not whether instantiation succeeded—True means we started it and should close it later.
explanations.133: The second parameter False means 'don't automatically update links in the spreadsheet' when opening the file.
explanations.156: GetFileName extracts just the filename (like 'book.xlsm') from a full path, which is needed because Excel's Workbooks collection uses basenames, not full paths.
explanations.196: Right(ref.FullPath,5) grabs the last 5 characters of the file path to check if it ends with '.xlam'.
explanations.239: Right(WScript.FullName, 12) extracts the last 12 characters to see if the script engine is cscript.exe, since that's that many characters long.
explanations.244: Chr(34) is the double-quote character, used here to wrap command-line arguments so spaces inside them don't break the command.
explanations.252: The second parameter 1 activates the window when the command runs, and the third parameter true keeps it open instead of closing immediately.
explanations.280: THen is a typo—should be Then—but VBScript is case-insensitive so it still works.
